DEMURRER to CROSS COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Apr-04-2022 Overruled
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. SOPHIE O. LAFRANCHI, Esq. of Manning & Kass Ellrod, Ramirez, Trester LLP (Tel: 415-217-6990) for defendant/cross-complainant Uber Technologies, Inc.
Atty. KENDRA E. MCGEE, Esq. of San Jose City Attorney's Office (Tel: 408-535-1900) for cross-defendant City of San Jose.

HEARING RE: CROSS-DEFENDANT CITY OF SAN JOSE DEMURRER TO CROSS-COMPLAINT.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

The City of San Jose's demurrer to Uber Technologies, Inc.'s cross-complaint is overruled. Pedestrians Teresa Jacob and Louella Belandres were struck in a motor vehicle incident in November 2018; the former was killed, the latter injured. Two lawsuits were filed over the same event - in Santa Clara County Superior Court (Jacobs) and in our court (Belandres).
In January 2020, San Jose filed an indemnity and contribution cross-complaint against Uber in Jacobs. In October 2021, Uber filed an indemnity cross-complaint against San Jose in our case. San Jose demurs on the ground that Uber did not plead compliance with the government claims act. However, such claim-filing is not required where, as here, the cross-claim responds to a government entity's cross-claim, the same event is at issue and the cross-claim is not for affirmative relief. (Krainock v. Sup. Ct. (1990) 216 Cal.App.3d 1473, 1477-79.)
The requests for judicial notice are granted.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and comply with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR No. 8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/RBU)

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

CROSS-DEFENDANT CITY OF SAN JOSE'S DEMURRER to CROSS COMPLAINT is ordered off calendar for noncompliance with CCP 430.41. HEARING RE: DEFENDANT ANTHONY RAB's Motion For Protective Order Re His Deposition.

Parties stipulate to the matter being decided by the pro tem judge. The pro tem judge and counsel for parties (all appearing remotely) authorize the clerk to sign the formal stipulation form on their behalf.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Motion for protective order DENIED. Rab did not effectively serve an objection as required by CCP 2025.410. This is sufficient to deny the motion. While it is unseemly for Uber to argue to the Superior Court of Santa Clara County that the two actions are so similar that the cases should be consolidated and at the same time to argue to this Court that the cases are different enough that it should be entitled to take further deposition testimony, CCP 2025.610 and 2025.620 specifically apply only to "the action." Moreover, it is unclear that Evidence Code 1291 would apply because Rab is not "unavailable" as that term is used in the Evidence Code. See Assembly Committee Comment to Evidence Code 1291.

The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order and must e-mail it to the pro tem judge at sborrowman@gmail.com.
